verses.text: Solomon loves many foreign women — Moabite, Ammonite, Edomite, and more — taking seven hundred wives and three hundred concubines, against God's clear word. The wisest of men ignores the wisdom he himself wrote. No gift of grace makes us safe to indulge a known sin.

Solomon loves many foreign women — Moabite, Ammonite, Edomite, and more — taking seven hundred wives and three hundred concubines, against God's clear word. The wisest of men ignores the wisdom he himself wrote. No gift of grace makes us safe to indulge a known sin.

Solomon loves many foreign women — Moabite, Ammonite, Edomite, and more — taking seven hundred wives and three hundred concubines, against God's clear word. The wisest of men ignores the wisdom he himself wrote. No gift of grace makes us safe to indulge a known sin.

In his old age, his wives turn his heart after other gods, and 'his heart was not perfect with the LORD.' The decline is gradual but real. Small compromises long allowed at last divide the heart; the man who built God's temple now bows toward idols.

In his old age, his wives turn his heart after other gods, and 'his heart was not perfect with the LORD.' The decline is gradual but real. Small compromises long allowed at last divide the heart; the man who built God's temple now bows toward idols.

In his old age, his wives turn his heart after other gods, and 'his heart was not perfect with the LORD.' The decline is gradual but real. Small compromises long allowed at last divide the heart; the man who built God's temple now bows toward idols.

He even builds high places for Chemosh and Molech to please his wives. The king who once prayed at the temple now raises shrines to demons. Love misplaced will build altars to whatever it serves; the heart always worships something.

He even builds high places for Chemosh and Molech to please his wives. The king who once prayed at the temple now raises shrines to demons. Love misplaced will build altars to whatever it serves; the heart always worships something.

God is angry, for He had appeared to Solomon twice, and declares the kingdom will be torn away — yet for David's sake, and Jerusalem's, not in his days, and one tribe left to his son. Judgment is sure, but tempered by covenant mercy to David's line.

God stirs up Hadad the Edomite, a survivor of David's old wars, as an adversary. The peace Solomon enjoyed begins to crack. When a heart turns from God, the hedge of protection lifts, and old enemies, long quiet, rise again.

God stirs up Hadad the Edomite, a survivor of David's old wars, as an adversary. The peace Solomon enjoyed begins to crack. When a heart turns from God, the hedge of protection lifts, and old enemies, long quiet, rise again.

Hadad had fled to Egypt as a child, found favour with Pharaoh, and now returns to trouble Israel. God had preserved this thorn for the appointed hour. The LORD keeps His instruments of discipline ready, raising them up exactly when His people need correcting.

Rezon too becomes an adversary in Syria, hating Israel all Solomon's days. A second thorn is added. The reign that began in unbroken peace now bristles with enemies on every side — the visible fruit of an invisible turning from God.

Jeroboam, an able young officer Solomon promoted, is introduced — the man who will tear away the ten tribes. God often raises the agent of judgment from within, from among those a man himself advanced. The seeds of a kingdom's loss can grow in its own court.

The prophet Ahijah meets Jeroboam, tears a new garment into twelve pieces, and gives him ten: God will rend the kingdom from Solomon for forsaking Him and worshiping idols. The torn cloak preaches the torn kingdom. Sin's consequences are dramatized so none can mistake them.

Yet God will not take all in Solomon's day, leaving one tribe for David's sake and His chosen city, and He offers Jeroboam a sure house if he will obey. Even in judgment, mercy and covenant hold; the new king is given the same condition — walk with God, and be established.

Yet God will not take all in Solomon's day, leaving one tribe for David's sake and His chosen city, and He offers Jeroboam a sure house if he will obey. Even in judgment, mercy and covenant hold; the new king is given the same condition — walk with God, and be established.

But Solomon, hearing of it, seeks to kill Jeroboam, who flees to Egypt till Solomon dies. The king fights the very word of God rather than repent. When men will not turn from sin, they end up resisting God Himself, trying to kill what He has decreed.

But Solomon, hearing of it, seeks to kill Jeroboam, who flees to Egypt till Solomon dies. The king fights the very word of God rather than repent. When men will not turn from sin, they end up resisting God Himself, trying to kill what He has decreed.

But Solomon, hearing of it, seeks to kill Jeroboam, who flees to Egypt till Solomon dies. The king fights the very word of God rather than repent. When men will not turn from sin, they end up resisting God Himself, trying to kill what He has decreed.

Solomon's long reign ends; he dies and is buried, and Rehoboam reigns in his place. The wisest, richest king passes into the grave like all before him, his glory dimmed by his fall. Earthly greatness fades; only faithfulness to God endures beyond death.
